title: Modern History Signals
summary: Modern history signals show how the recent past keeps shaping present debates about power, memory, and identity.

items.name: Digital archives are expanding
items.whyItMatters: Access changes who can study history and how quickly.

items.name: Memory politics remain active
items.summary: Public debates about monuments, curriculum, and commemoration continue.
items.whyItMatters: History still matters in contemporary identity disputes.

items.name: Publishing houses
items.summary: History books still shape public understanding.
items.whyItMatters: The pipeline from scholarship to public conversation still matters.

items.name: Documentary platforms
items.summary: Video media increasingly mediates historical knowledge.
items.whyItMatters: History now competes in attention markets.

items.name: Archives
items.summary: Records are being digitised and remixed.
items.whyItMatters: Preserving evidence is itself a political act.

items.name: Museums
items.summary: Exhibitions are becoming more interpretive and inclusive.

items.name: Margaret MacMillan
items.summary: A major public historian of war and diplomacy.
items.whyItMatters: She keeps modern history legible to broad audiences.

items.name: Tony Judt
items.summary: A key interpreter of postwar Europe.
items.whyItMatters: His work remains central to the study of the modern order.

items.name: Whose history gets told?
items.summary: Public debate increasingly focuses on marginalised voices.
items.whyItMatters: The historical canon is being rebalanced.

items.name: Can archives ever be neutral?
items.summary: Selection and preservation shape the story before interpretation begins.
items.whyItMatters: This changes how historians think about authority.
